en.extract:
The crested serpent eagle (Spilornis cheela) is a medium-sized bird of prey that is found in forested habitats across tropical Asia. Within its widespread range across the Indian subcontinent, Southeast Asia and East Asia, there are considerable variations and some authorities prefer to treat several of its subspecies as completely separate species. In the past, several species including the Philippine serpent eagle (S. holospila), Andaman serpent eagle (S. elgini) and South Nicobar serpent eagle (S. klossi) were treated as subspecies of the Crested serpent eagle. All members within the species complex have a large looking head with long feathers on the back of the head giving them a maned and crested appearance. The face is bare and yellow joining up with the ceres while the powerful feet are unfeathered and heavily scaled. They fly over the forest canopy on broad wings and tail have wide white and black bars. They call often with a loud, piercing and familiar three or two-note call. They often feed on snakes, giving them their name and are placed along with the Circaetus snake-eagles in the subfamily Circaetinae.


Description

This medium-large, dark brown eagle is stocky, with rounded wings and a short tail. Its short black and white fan-shaped nuchal crest gives it a thick-necked appearance. The bare facial skin and feet are yellow. The underside is spotted with white and yellowish-brown. When perched the wing tips do not reach until the tail tip. In soaring flight, the broad and paddle-shaped wings are held in a shallow V. The tail and underside of the flight feathers are black with broad white bars. Young birds show a lot of white on the head. The tarsus is unfeathered and covered by hexagonal scales. The upper mandible does not have an overhanging festoon to the tip. 


Size
This species of serpent eagle manifests an unusual amount of size variation across its assorted subspecies. Total length of fully-grown crested serpent eagles can vary from 41 to 75 cm (16 to 30 in) and wingspan can vary from 89 to 169 cm (2 ft 11 in to 5 ft 7 in). The largest proportioned race appears to be the nominate, S. c. cheela, with a wing chord of 468 to 510 mm (18.4 to 20.1 in) in males and 482 to 532 mm (19.0 to 20.9 in) in females, tail lengths of 295 to 315 mm (11.6 to 12.4 in) and tarsus length of 100 to 115 mm (3.9 to 4.5 in). In comparison, S. c. minimus, probably the smallest race, has a wing chord of 257 to 291 mm (10.1 to 11.5 in) in males and 288 to 304 mm (11.3 to 12.0 in) in females, tail lengths of around 191 mm (7.5 in) and tarsal length of around 76 mm (3.0 in). Weights are more fitfully reported but are estimated to vary perhaps threefold between assorted races. In very small subspecies such as S. c. asturinus body masses were found to be 420 g (15 oz) in a male and 565 g (19.9 oz) in a female.  In S. c. palawanesis, body mass was reported at 688 g (24.3 oz) in males and 853 g (30.1 oz) in females. Serpent eagles from Borneo, S. c. pallidus, may between 625 and 1,130 g (22.0 and 39.9 oz). Mainland forms are typically larger but a weight of 900 g (32 oz) was cited for the fairly small  mainland race, S. c. burmanicus. In the race S. c. hoya, weights were much higher, averaging 1,207 g (42.6 oz); meanwhile, in the same race, 8 males averaged 1,539 g (54.3 oz) and 6 females averaged 1,824 g (64.3 oz). In some cases, serpent eagles may attain estimated weights of approximately 2,300 g (81 oz).


Taxonomy

The crested serpent eagle is placed along with the snake eagles of the genus Circaetus in the subfamily Circaetinae.
The nominate subspecies has a black throat while the peninsular Indian form has a brownish throat. There are clinal latitudinal variations, with size decreasing southward. The small islands taxa are generally smaller in size than the taxa from the Asian mainland/larger islands in a phenomenon termed as insular dwarfism.
Within its widespread range across tropical Asia, 21 subspecies have been proposed:

S. c. batu from southern Sumatra and Batu,
S. c. bido from Java and Bali,
S. c. burmanicus in most of Indochina,
S. c. hoya from Taiwan,
S. c. malayensis of the Thai-Malay Peninsula and northern Sumatra,
S. c. melanotis in Peninsular India,
S. c. palawanensis from Palawan,
S. c. pallidus from northern Borneo,
S. c. richmondi from southern Borneo,
S. c. ricketti in northern Vietnam and southern China,
S. c. rutherfordi from Hainan, and
S. c. spilogaster of Sri Lanka.
The remaining sub-species are all restricted to smaller islands: 

S. c. abbotti (Simeulue serpent eagle) from Simeulue,
S. c. asturinus (Nias serpent eagle) from Nias,
S. c. baweanus (Bawean serpent eagle) of the Bawean,
S. c. davisoni in the Andamans,
S. c. minimus (Central Nicobar serpent eagle) from the central Nicobars,
S. c. natunensis (Natuna serpent eagle) from Natuna,
S. c. perplexus (Ryukyu serpent eagle) from Ryukyu, and
S. c. sipora (Mentawai serpent eagle) from Mentawai.
The last seven (with English names in brackets) are sometimes treated as separate species. Although the crested serpent eagle remains widespread and fairly common overall, some of the taxa that are restricted to small islands are believed to have relatively small populations that likely are in the hundreds. 
The rarest is probably the Bawean serpent eagle with a declining population of about 26–37 pairs, which makes it critically endangered.


Behaviour and ecology

The crested serpent eagle is a reptile eater which hunts over forests, often close to wet grassland, for snakes and lizards. It has also been observed to prey on birds, large insects like grasshoppers or locusts, amphibians, mammals, fishes, termites and large earthworms. It is found mainly over areas with thick vegetation both on the low hills and the plains. This species is a resident species, but in some parts of their range they are found only in summer.
The call is a distinctive Kluee-wip-wip with the first note being high and rising. They call a lot in the late mornings from their perches where they spend a lot of time and they rise on thermals in the mornings. In southern Taiwan, males have a larger home range than females. Males on average had a home range of 16.7 km2 while females used about 7 km2. When alarmed, they erect the crest and the head appears large and framed by the ruff. They will sometimes follow snakes on the ground. They roost in the interiors of trees with dense foliage. A radio-telemetric study of the species in Taiwan found that the birds spend 98% of the day perched and usually finding food in the morning hours. They appear to use a sit and wait foraging strategy.

The breeding season begins in late winter when they start courting and establishing territories. The eggs are laid in early summer. Old nests are often refurbished and reused in India but a study in Penang found them to build fresh nests each year. A study in India found that most nests were built along riverine trees. The nest is a large platform built high on a tree. Both birds in a pair build the nest but the female alone incubates. The male guards when the female forages. In central India, the Terminalia tomentosa is often used while Terminalia bellirica and Dalbergia latifolia was often used in southern India. In Penang, the nest trees were typically large and isolated from other trees with lot of room for the birds to fly in and out. The nests are lined with green leaves collected from nearby and are placed facing down on the nest floor. The usual clutch is one egg but two are sometimes laid and only a single chick is successfully raised in a season. When eggs are lost, a replacement is laid two to seven weeks later. The eggs hatch after about 41 days and the young fledge after about two months. Nests are defended by the parents.

Several species of endoparasitic nematodes have been recovered from the intestines of crested serpent eagles, including Madelinema angelae. Avian pox virus infections which cause warts on the face have been observed in a wild bird living in Taiwan. A number of ectoparasitic bird lice have been described from the species including Kurodaia cheelae. In Penang, it was found that cinereous tits (Parus cinereus ambiguus) tended to nest close to nesting crested serpent eagles, presumably due to safety from predators like crows that may be driven off by the eagles. They have also been found to visit the nests of the eagles to collect fur from the remains of dead mammals.


References


External links

Photographs and videos
Call recordings

Within its widespread range across the [[Indian subcontinent]], [[Southeast Asia]] and [[East Asia]], there are considerable variations and some authorities prefer to treat several of its [[subspecies]] as completely separate species.Ferguson-Lees, James & Christie, David A. (2001). ''Raptors of the World.'' Christopher Helm, London. {{ISBN|0-7136-8026-1}} In the past, several species including the [[Philippine serpent eagle]] (''S. holospila''), [[Andaman serpent eagle]] (''S. elgini'') and [[South Nicobar serpent eagle]] (''S. klossi'') were treated as subspecies of the Crested serpent eagle. All members within the [[species complex]] have a large looking head with long feathers on the back of the head giving them a maned and crested appearance. The face is bare and yellow joining up with the [[cere]]s while the powerful feet are unfeathered and heavily scaled. They fly over the forest canopy on broad wings and tail have wide white and black bars. They call often with a loud, piercing and familiar three or two-note call. They often feed on snakes, giving them their name and are placed along with the ''[[Circaetus]]'' snake-eagles in the subfamily [[Circaetinae]]. ==Description== [[File:Crested serpent-eagle (Spilornis cheela spilogaster).jpg|thumb|''S. c, spilogaster''
[[Yala National Park]], [[Sri Lanka]]]] This medium-large, dark brown eagle is stocky, with rounded wings and a short tail. Its short black and white fan-shaped nuchal crest gives it a thick-necked appearance. The bare facial skin and feet are yellow. The underside is spotted with white and yellowish-brown. When perched the wing tips do not reach until the tail tip. In soaring flight, the broad and paddle-shaped wings are held in a shallow V. The tail and underside of the flight [[feather]]s are black with broad white bars. Young birds show a lot of white on the head.{{cite book |author1=Rasmussen, P.C. |author2=Anderton, J.C. |year=2005 |title=Birds of South Asia: The Ripley Guide |volume=2 |publisher= Smithsonian Institution & Lynx Edicions |pages=92–93}}{{cite book |url=https://archive.org/stream/faunaofbritishin03oate#page/357/mode/1up|pages=357–360|author=Blanford, W.T. | title=The Fauna of British India, including Ceylon and Burma. Birds. Volume 3|year=1895|publisher=Taylor and Francis|place=London}} The tarsus is unfeathered and covered by hexagonal scales. The upper mandible does not have an overhanging festoon to the tip.{{cite book|author1=Ali, S. |author2=Ripley, S.D. |title=Handbook of the Birds of India and Pakistan |volume=1 |edition=Second |publisher=Oxford University Press |place=New Delhi|year=1978|pages=329–334}} ===Size=== This species of serpent eagle manifests an unusual amount of size variation across its assorted subspecies. Total length of fully-grown crested serpent eagles can vary from {{cvt|41|to|75|cm}} and wingspan can vary from {{cvt|89|to|169|cm|ftin}}.Clark, W.S., J. S. Marks, and G. M. Kirwan (2020). Princeton University Press.{{cite book |last1=Ferguson-Lees |first1=J. |last2=Christie |first2=D. |year=2001 |title=''Raptors of the World'' |publisher=Houghton Mifflin Harcourt |isbn=0-618-12762-3}} The largest proportioned race appears to be the nominate, ''S. c. cheela'', with a [[Wing chord (biology)|wing chord]] of {{cvt|468|to|510|mm}} in males and {{cvt|482|to|532|mm}} in females, tail lengths of {{cvt|295|to|315|mm}} and tarsus length of {{cvt|100|to|115|mm}}. In comparison, ''S. c. minimus'', probably the smallest race, has a wing chord of {{cvt|257|to|291|mm}} in males and {{cvt|288|to|304|mm}} in females, tail lengths of around {{cvt|191|mm}} and tarsal length of around {{cvt|76|mm}}. Weights are more fitfully reported but are estimated to vary perhaps threefold between assorted races.{{cite book |title=CRC Handbook of Avian Body Masses |edition=2nd |editor-first=John B. Jr. |editor-last=Dunning |publisher=CRC Press |year=2008 |isbn=978-1-4200-6444-5}} In very small subspecies such as ''S. c. asturinus'' body masses were found to be {{cvt|420|g}} in a male and {{cvt|565|g}} in a female. In ''S. c. palawanesis'', body mass was reported at {{cvt|688|g}} in males and {{cvt|853|g}} in females.Gamauf, A., Preleuthner, M., & Winkler, H. (1998). ''Philippine birds of prey: interrelations among habitat, morphology, and behavior''. The Auk, 115(3), 713-726. Serpent eagles from Borneo, ''S. c. pallidus'', may between {{cvt|625|and|1130|g}}.Artuti, A. K., Sari, M., Retnaningtyas, R. W., & Listyorini, D. (2020, November). ''A phylogenetic analysis of Crested Serpent Eagle (Spilornis cheela) based on cytochrome-c oxydase subunit I (COI): a stepping stone towards genetic conservation of raptors in Indonesia''. In IOP Conference Series: Earth and Environmental Science (Vol. 590, No. 1, p. 012008). IOP Publishing. Mainland forms are typically larger but a weight of {{cvt|900|g}} was cited for the fairly small mainland race, ''S. c. burmanicus''.Brown, L. & Amadon, D. (1986) ''Eagles, Hawks and Falcons of the World''. The Wellfleet Press. {{ISBN|978-1555214722}}. In the race ''S. c. hoya'', weights were much higher, averaging {{cvt|1207|g}}; meanwhile, in the same race, 8 males averaged {{cvt|1539|g}} and 6 females averaged {{cvt|1824|g}}.Tsai, P. Y., Ko, C. J., Hsieh, C., Su, Y. T., Lu, Y. J., Lin, R. S., & Tuanmu, M. N. (2020). A trait dataset for Taiwan's breeding birds. Biodiversity data journal, 8.Chou, T. C., Walther, B. A., & Lee, P. F. (2012). ''Spacing pattern of the Crested Serpent-eagle (spilornis cheela hoya) in Southern Taiwan''. Taiwania, 57(1), 1-13. In some cases, serpent eagles may attain estimated weights of approximately {{cvt|2300|g}}.Unwin, M., & Tipling, D. (2018). ''The Empire of the Eagle: An Illustrated Natural History''. Yale University Press. ==Taxonomy== [[File:Spilornis cheela immature.jpg|thumb|Immature ''S. c. perplexus''
[[Iriomote]], [[Okinawa Prefecture|Okinawa]]]] The crested serpent eagle is placed along with the snake eagles of the genus ''Circaetus'' in the subfamily Circaetinae.{{cite journal |doi=10.1016/j.ympev.2005.04.010 |title=Phylogeny of eagles, Old World vultures, and other Accipitridae based on nuclear and mitochondrial DNA|year=2005|last1=Lerner|first1=H.R.L. |last2=Mindell|first2=D.P. |journal=Molecular Phylogenetics and Evolution |volume=37|issue=2|pages=327–46|pmid=15925523|bibcode=2005MolPE..37..327L }} The nominate subspecies has a black throat while the peninsular Indian form has a brownish throat. There are clinal latitudinal variations, with size decreasing southward. The small islands taxa are generally smaller in size than the taxa from the Asian mainland/larger islands in a phenomenon termed as [[insular dwarfism]]. Within its widespread range across tropical Asia, 21 subspecies have been proposed: * ''S. c. batu'' from southern Sumatra and [[Batu Islands|Batu]], * ''S. c. bido'' from [[Java]] and [[Bali]], * ''S. c. burmanicus'' in most of [[Indochina]], * ''S. c. hoya'' from [[Taiwan]], * ''S. c. malayensis'' of the [[Thai-Malay Peninsula]] and northern [[Sumatra]], * ''S. c. melanotis'' in [[Peninsular India]], * ''S. c. palawanensis'' from [[Palawan]], * ''S. c. pallidus'' from northern [[Borneo]], * ''S. c. richmondi'' from southern Borneo, * ''S. c. ricketti'' in northern [[Vietnam]] and southern [[China]], * ''S. c. rutherfordi'' from [[Hainan]], and * ''S. c. spilogaster'' of Sri Lanka. The remaining sub-species are all restricted to smaller islands: * ''S. c. abbotti'' (Simeulue serpent eagle) from [[Simeulue]], * ''S. c. asturinus'' (Nias serpent eagle) from [[Nias]], * ''S. c. baweanus'' (Bawean serpent eagle) of the [[Bawean]], * ''S. c. davisoni'' in the [[Andamans]], * ''S. c. minimus'' (Central Nicobar serpent eagle) from the central [[Nicobars]], * ''S. c. natunensis'' (Natuna serpent eagle) from [[Natuna]], * ''S. c. perplexus'' (Ryukyu serpent eagle) from [[Ryukyu]], and * ''S. c. sipora'' (Mentawai serpent eagle) from [[Mentawai Islands Regency|Mentawai]]. The last seven (with English names in brackets) are sometimes treated as separate species. Although the crested serpent eagle remains widespread and fairly common overall, some of the [[taxa]] that are restricted to small islands are believed to have relatively small populations that likely are in the hundreds. The rarest is probably the Bawean serpent eagle with a declining population of about 26–37 pairs, which makes it [[critically endangered]].{{cite journal |author=Nijman, V.|year=2006| title=The endemic Bawean Serpent-eagle ''Spilornis baweanus'': habitat use, abundance and conservation |journal= Bird Conservation International |volume=16 |issue=2 |pages=131–143 |doi=10.1017/S0959270906000219|doi-access=free}}{{cite book|title=Check-List of Birds of the World. Volume 1|edition=Second |editor1=Mayr, E. |editor2=Cottrell, G.W. | year=1979| publisher=Museum of Comparative Zoology| place= Cambridge, Massachusetts |pages=311–315 |url=https://archive.org/stream/checklistofbirds12pete#page/311/mode/1up/}}{{cite journal |author=Oberholser, H.C. |title= The birds of the Natuna Islands|journal=Bulletin of the United States National Museum|volume=159| pages=18–21 |year=1923 |url=https://archive.org/stream/bulletinunitedst1591932unit#page/18/mode/1up}}{{cite journal |journal=Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club|volume=38| year=1918 |issue=245 |author=Sclater, W.L. |pages=37–41|url=https://archive.org/stream/bulletinofb3840191720brit#page/37/mode/1up/|title=Descriptions of new hawks}} ==Behaviour and ecology== [[File:Crested serpent eagle - (Spilornis cheela) 1.jpg|thumb|Crested serpent eagle]] The crested serpent eagle is a [[reptile]] eater which hunts over forests, often close to wet grassland,{{cite journal |author1=Ueta, M. |author2=Minton, J.S. |year=1996| journal=Journal of Raptor Research |volume=30 |issue=2 |pages=99–100 |title=Habitat preference of Crested Serpent Eagles in Southern Japan |url=http://sora.unm.edu/sites/default/files/journals/jrr/v030n02/p00099-p00100.pdf}} for [[snake]]s and [[lizard]]s. It has also been observed to prey on birds, large insects like grasshoppers or locusts, amphibians, mammals, fishes, termites and large earthworms.{{cite web | url=https://www.peregrinefund.org/explore-raptors-species/eagles/crested-serpent-eagle | title=Crested Serpent-Eagle | the Peregrine Fund }} It is found mainly over areas with thick vegetation both on the low hills and the plains. This species is a resident species, but in some parts of their range they are found only in summer.{{cite journal |title=Attempt by the crested Serpent Eagle ''Spilornis cheela'' to seize the Indian cobra ''Naja naja'' |author=Purandare, K. | journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |year= 2002| volume = 99| issue=2 |page=299 |url=https://biodiversitylibrary.org/page/48604197}} The call is a distinctive ''Kluee-wip-wip'' with the first note being high and rising. They call a lot in the late mornings from their perches where they spend a lot of time and they rise on [[thermal]]s in the mornings. In southern Taiwan, males have a larger home range than females. Males on average had a home range of 16.7 km2 while females used about 7 km2.{{cite journal |title=Spacing Pattern of the Crested Serpent Eagle (''Spilornis cheela hoya'') in Southern Taiwan |author1=Chou, T. |author2=Walther, B.A. |author3=Pei-Fen Lee |journal=Taiwania |volume=57 |issue=1 |pages=1–13 |year=2012 |url=http://tai2.ntu.edu.tw/taiwania/pdf/tai.2012.57.1.1.pdf|access-date=2013-02-05|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20131203001812/http://tai2.ntu.edu.tw/taiwania/pdf/tai.2012.57.1.1.pdf|archive-date=2013-12-03}} When alarmed, they erect the crest and the head appears large and framed by the ruff. They will sometimes follow snakes on the ground. They roost in the interiors of trees with dense foliage.{{cite journal|author=Baker, E.C.S. |title=Some notes on tame Serpent Eagles|pages=154–159 |journal=The Avicultural Magazine|volume=5| issue=5| year=1914 |url=https://archive.org/stream/avicultural5319131914avic#page/154/mode/1up/}} A radio-telemetric study of the species in Taiwan found that the birds spend 98% of the day perched and usually finding food in the morning hours. They appear to use a sit and wait foraging strategy.{{cite thesis |url=http://140.133.6.46/ETD-db/ETD-search/view_etd?URN=etd-0124111-145536 |title=Diurnal activity pattern of Crested Serpent Eagles ''Spilornis cheela hoya'' in Kenting, southern Taiwan |author=Chia-hong, L. |year=2010 |publisher=Graduate Institute of Environment and Ecology |place=Taiwan |language=Chinese |archive-date=2012-04-02 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120402110118/http://140.133.6.46/ETD-db/ETD-search/view_etd?URN=etd-0124111-145536}} [[File:Feather structure of Crested Serpent Eagle.JPG|thumb|In flight the wide white band is distinctive]] The breeding season begins in late winter when they start courting and establishing territories. The eggs are laid in early summer. Old nests are often refurbished and reused in India but a study in Penang found them to build fresh nests each year. A study in India found that most nests were built along riverine trees. The nest is a large platform built high on a tree. Both birds in a pair build the nest but the female alone incubates. The male guards when the female forages. In central India, the ''[[Terminalia tomentosa]]'' is often used while ''[[Terminalia bellirica]]'' and ''[[Dalbergia latifolia]]'' was often used in southern India.{{cite journal|journal=Taprobanica |year= 2012|volume=4| issue=2| pages=77–82| title=Breeding ecology of the crested serpent eagle ''Spilornis cheela'' (Latham, 1790) (Aves: Accipitriformes: Accipitridae) in Kolli Hills, Tamil Nadu, India |doi=10.4038/tapro.v4i2.5059 |last1= Gokula|first1= V. |doi-access=free}} In Penang, the nest trees were typically large and isolated from other trees with lot of room for the birds to fly in and out. The nests are lined with green leaves collected from nearby and are placed facing down on the nest floor.{{cite journal|author1=Naoroji, R. K. |author2=Monga, S.G. |year=1983| title=Observations on the Crested Serpent Eagle (''Spilornis cheela'') in Rajpipla forests – South Gujarat |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=80 |issue=2 |pages=273–285 |url=https://archive.org/details/journalofbombay801983bomb/page/n299/mode/2up}} The usual clutch is one egg but two are sometimes laid and only a single chick is successfully raised in a season. When eggs are lost, a replacement is laid two to seven weeks later. The eggs hatch after about 41 days and the young fledge after about two months. Nests are defended by the parents.{{cite journal |author=Daly, W. M. |year=1895 |title=The southern Indian harrier eagle |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=9 |issue=4 |page=487 |url=https://archive.org/details/journalofbombayn9189495bomb/page/487/mode/1up}}{{cite journal| author= Osman, S.M. |year=1972 |title=The Crested Serpent Eagle |journal=Journal of the Bombay Natural History Society |volume=69 |issue=3 |pages=461–468 |url=https://archive.org/details/journalofbo6943531972bomb/page/n591/mode/2up}}{{cite book |title=The nests and eggs of Indian birds. Volume 3|author=Hume, A.O. |year=1890 |publisher=R.H. Porter |place=London |url=https://archive.org/stream/nestseggsofindia03humerich#page/152/mode/2up/search/cheela|pages=152–154}} [[File:Crested Serpent Eagle (Spilornis Cheela) at Kaziranga National Park.jpg|thumb|The crested serpent eagle at Kaziranga National Park]] Several species of endoparasitic nematodes have been recovered from the intestines of crested serpent eagles, including ''Madelinema angelae''.{{cite journal|title= Nematode Parasites of Oceanica. XI. ''Madelinema angelae'' gen. et sp. n., and ''Inglisonema mawsonae'' sp. n. (Heterakoidea: Inglisonematidae) from birds |author1=Schmidt, G.D. |author2=Kuntz, R.E. |journal=Journal of Parasitology |volume=57 |issue=3 |year=1971 |pages=479–484 |doi=10.2307/3277897|pmid= 5104559 |jstor=3277897|doi-access=free }}{{cite journal |author1=Yoshino, T. |author2=Shingake, T. |author3=Onuma, M. |author4=Kinjo, T. |author5=Yanai, T. |author6=Fukushi, H. |author7=Kuwana, T. |author8=Asakawa, M. |year=2010| title= Parasitic helminths and arthropods of the Crested Serpent Eagle ''Spilornis cheela perplexus'' Swann, 1922 from the Yaeyama |journal=Journal of the Yamashina Institute for Ornithology| volume=41| issue=1| pages=55–61 |doi=10.3312/jyio.41.55 |doi-access=free}} Avian pox virus infections which cause warts on the face have been observed in a wild bird living in Taiwan.{{cite journal|journal=Avian Dis. |year=2011| volume=55| issue=1| pages=143–146| title=Avian pox infection in a free-living crested serpent eagle (Spilornis cheela) in southern Taiwan |author1=Chen C.C., Pei, K.J., Lee, F.R., Tzeng, M.P., Chang, T.C. |doi=10.1637/9510-082610-Case.1 |pmid=21500652|s2cid=956795}} A number of ectoparasitic bird lice have been described from the species including ''Kurodaia cheelae''.{{cite journal|title=The Genus Kurodaia (Mallophaga: Menoponidae) from the Falconiformes, with Elevation of the Subgenus ''Falcomenopon'' to Generic Rank |author1=Price, R.D. |author2=Beer, J.R. | journal= Annals of the Entomological Society of America| volume= 56| issue=3| year=1963 |pages=379–385 |doi=10.1093/aesa/56.3.379}} In Penang, it was found that [[cinereous tit]]s (''Parus cinereus ambiguus'') tended to nest close to nesting crested serpent eagles, presumably due to safety from predators like crows that may be driven off by the eagles. They have also been found to visit the nests of the eagles to collect fur from the remains of dead mammals.{{cite journal|title=The serpent eagles ''Spilornis cheela'' of Penang Island, Malaya|author=Cairns, J. |year=1968| journal=Ibis |volume=110 |issue=4| pages=569–571|doi=10.1111/j.1474-919X.1968.tb00064.x}} == References == {{Reflist}} ==External links== {{Commons category|Spilornis cheela}} {{Wikispecies|Spilornis cheela}} * [http://ibc.lynxeds.com/species/crested-serpent-eagle-spilornis-cheela Photographs and videos] * [https://archive.today/20130416064314/http://www.xeno-canto.org/XCspeciesprofiles.php?species_nr2=8219.00&pagenumber=&order=taxonomy&view=3 Call recordings] {{Circaetinae}} {{Taxonbar|from=Q431212}} {{Authority control}} [[Category:Spilornis|crested serpent eagle]] [[Category:Articles containing video clips]] [[Category:Eagles|crested serpent eagle]] [[Category:Birds of Indomalaya]] [[Category:Birds described in 1790|crested serpent eagle]]

zh.extract:
大冠鷲（學名：Spilornis cheela）（西拉雅語：Akakul）又名冠蛇鵰、冕蛇鵰、食蛇鵰，粵語俗稱食蛇鷹，是一種中大型猛禽，屬於鷹科一員。分布於熱帶亞洲的森林棲地中。在它廣泛分布的印度次大陸、東南亞和東亞範圍內，存在顯著的變異，一些權威機構更傾向於將其數個亞種視為完全獨立的物種。 過去，包括菲律賓蛇鵰（S. holospila）、安達曼蛇鵰（S. elgini）和南尼科巴蛇鵰（S. klossi）在內的數個物種被視為大冠鷲的亞種。所有複合種內的成員均擁有看似龐大的頭部，頭後部的長羽毛賦予它們鬃毛和冠狀的外觀。臉部裸露且呈黃色，與蠟膜相連，而強健的腳則無羽毛覆蓋且有厚重的鱗片。牠們以寬廣的翅膀和尾巴飛越森林樹冠，翼和尾上有寬闊的白色與黑色條紋。牠們經常發出響亮、刺耳且熟悉的兩或三音節叫聲。牠們經常以蛇為食，因此得名，並與短趾鵰屬（Circaetus）一起歸類於蛇鵰亞科。


描述

大冠鷲身長約55至75公分，翼展約150至170公分，成鳥背面和後頸為黑褐色，胸部和腹部和翼下為淡褐色，胸腹有白色斑點，停棲時可見明顯冠羽，在高空時翼開展保持以淺Ｖ字型翱翔，尾巴和翼下後緣為黑色有一條白色橫帶。
這種中大型的深褐色鵰體型壯碩，翼圓短而尾巴短。其短小的黑白扇形枕冠使其脖子顯得粗壯。裸露的臉部皮膚和腳皆為黃色。腹部帶有白色和黃褐色斑點。當站立時，翼尖不及尾尖。飛行時，寬闊的槳狀翅膀呈淺V形。尾部和飛行羽毛的下方為黑色，帶有寬廣的白色條紋。幼鳥頭部顯示大量白色。 跗蹠部無羽毛覆蓋，且被六角形鱗片覆蓋。上喙無向下彎曲的尖端。 


大小
這種蛇鵰的各個亞種之間表現出非同尋常的尺寸變異。成年大冠鷲的總長度可變動於41至75 cm（16至30英寸），翼展可變動於89至169 cm（2英尺11英寸至5英尺7英寸）。 最大的比例出現在指名種S. c. cheela中，雄鳥的翼弦長度為468至510 mm（18.4至20.1英寸），雌鳥為482至532 mm（19.0至20.9英寸），尾長為295至315 mm（11.6至12.4英寸），跗蹠長度為100至115 mm（3.9至4.5英寸）。相比之下，可能最小的亞種S. c. minimus，雄鳥的翼弦長度為257至291 mm（10.1至11.5英寸），雌鳥為288至304 mm（11.3至12.0英寸），尾長約191 mm（7.5英寸），跗蹠長度約76 mm（3.0英寸）。 體重的報告比較零星，但估計不同亞種之間的體重可能相差三倍之多。 在非常小的亞種如S. c. asturinus中，雄鳥的體重被發現為420 g（15 oz），雌鳥為565 g（19.9 oz）。 在S. c. palawanesis中，雄鳥體重為688 g（24.3 oz），雌鳥為853 g（30.1 oz）。 來自婆羅洲的大冠鷲，如S. c. pallidus，可能介於625、1,130 g（22.0、39.9 oz）之間。 大陸形式通常較大，但一個相對較小的大陸亞種S. c. burmanicus的體重被報導為900 g（32 oz）。 在S. c. hoya亞種中，體重更高，平均1,207 g（42.6 oz）。與此同時，同一亞種中的8隻雄性平均體重為1,539 g（54.3 oz），6隻雌性平均體重為1,824 g（64.3 oz）。 在某些情況下，大冠鷲的估計體重可達約2,300 g（81 oz）。


分類

大冠鷲與短趾鵰屬（Circaetus）的蛇鵰同屬於蛇鵰亞科。
指名亞種的喉部為黑色，而印度半島的形式則為棕色。隨著緯度的變化，存在著由北向南體型逐漸減少的漸變性變化。 小島嶼的種群通常比亞洲大陸/大島嶼的種群小，這是一種稱為島嶼侏儒化的現象。 在熱帶亞洲廣泛的分布範圍內，已提出21個亞種：

S. c. batu 來自蘇門答臘南部和巴圖，
S. c. bido 來自爪哇和峇里島，
S. c. burmanicus 分布於印度支那大部分地區，
S. c. hoya 來自台灣，
S. c. malayensis 來自泰國-馬來半島和蘇門答臘北部，
S. c. melanotis 分布於印度半島，
S. c. palawanensis 來自巴拉望，
S. c. pallidus 來自婆羅洲北部，
S. c. richmondi 來自婆羅洲南部，
S. c. ricketti 分布於越南北部和中國南部，
S. c. rutherfordi 來自海南島，以及
S. c. spilogaster 來自斯里蘭卡。
其餘亞種均限於小島嶼：

S. c. abbotti（錫默盧蛇鵰）來自錫默盧島，
S. c. asturinus（尼亞斯蛇鵰）來自尼亞斯島，
S. c. baweanus（巴威蛇鵰）來自巴威，
S. c. davisoni 分布於安達曼群島，
S. c. minimus（中央尼科巴蛇鵰）來自尼科巴群島中部，
S. c. natunensis（納土納蛇鵰）來自納土納群島，
S. c. perplexus（琉球蛇鵰）來自琉球，以及
S. c. sipora（明打威蛇鵰）來自明打威群島。
最後七個（括號內為英文名稱）有時被視為獨立的物種。 雖然大冠鷲整體上分布廣泛且相對常見，但有些僅限於小島嶼的種群被認為數量相對較少，可能僅有數百隻。 
其中最罕見的可能是巴威蛇鵰，其數量正在下降，約有26–37對，這使其成為極度瀕危物種。


分布
大冠鷲分佈很廣，在東亞、南亞、東南亞都有發現，從喜馬拉雅山到印度和斯里蘭卡到中國大陸南方和台灣及中南半島、菲律賓、印度尼西亞，築巢在森林近水邊的樹冠，鳥巢以樹枝條構成，且一巢只生一個蛋。


行為與生態

大冠鷲是一種爬行動物捕食者，經常在森林上空獵食，通常靠近濕潤的草地，捕食蛇和蜥蜴。它也被觀察到捕食鳥類、兩棲類、哺乳類、魚類、白蟻和大型蚯蚓。 它主要出現在低矮山丘和平原上有茂密植被的地區。此物種為留鳥，但在其分布區的某些地方，只在夏季出現。
其叫聲具有獨特的Kluee-wip-wip特徵，第一音符高且逐漸升高。牠們經常在早晨晚些時候從牠們的棲息處發出叫聲，並在早晨隨著熱氣流升空。 在台灣南部，雄性比雌性有較大的活動範圍。雄性的活動範圍平均為16.7平方公里，而雌性約為7平方公里。 當受到驚嚇時，牠們會豎起冠羽，使頭部顯得龐大且被頸毛環繞。 牠們有時會跟隨地上的蛇。 牠們棲息在樹葉繁茂的樹木內部。 在台灣對該物種進行的無線電追蹤研究發現，這些鳥類一天中的98%時間都在棲息，通常在早晨覓食。牠們似乎使用等待的方式來覓食。

繁殖季節始於冬末，當時牠們開始求偶並建立領域。牠們在初夏產卵。舊巢在印度經常被修復和重複使用，但在檳城的研究發現牠們每年都會建造新巢。 在印度的一項研究中發現，大多數巢位於河邊的樹木上。巢是一個建在高樹上的大平台。雌雄鳥會共同築巢，但只有雌鳥負責孵卵。雌鳥覓食時，雄鳥負責守護。在印度中部，毛欖仁（Terminalia tomentosa）經常被使用，而在印度南部，毗黎勒（Terminalia bellirica）和闊葉黃檀（Dalbergia latifolia）經常被使用。 在檳城，築巢的樹木通常很大且與其他樹木隔離，為鳥類進出提供了充足的空間。巢內鋪有從附近採集的綠葉，這些綠葉是倒置放在巢底的。 一般每次產一顆蛋，但有時會產兩顆蛋，每季僅成功撫育一隻雛鳥。如果蛋遺失，約兩到七週後會再產一顆蛋。蛋約在41天後孵化，幼鳥約兩個月後離巢。親鳥會保護巢穴。
在大冠鷲的腸道中發現了多種內寄生線蟲，包括Madelinema angelae。 在台灣一隻野生鳥類中觀察到了由禽痘病毒感染引起的面部疣狀病變。 從該物種中還描述了多種體外寄生的羽虱，包括Kurodaia cheelae。 在檳城，發現蒼背山雀（Parus cinereus ambiguus）傾向於在靠近大冠鷲巢的地方築巢，可能是因為鵰能驅趕如烏鴉等掠食者而帶來安全保障。它們還被發現會造訪鵰的巢，收集死去哺乳動物獵物遺留下來的毛皮。
